title: How Do Computers Learn? Training the Fraud Detector
description: Students compare explicit rules with machine learning, assign feature weights, and test a fraud model for accuracy and fairness trade-offs.

roleModelOfDay.name: Fei-Fei Li
roleModelOfDay.intro: A computer scientist who built ImageNet — a dataset of 14 million labelled images that transformed AI. She proved that the quality and diversity of data matters more than the cleverness of the algorithm.

vocabulary.def: A system that learns patterns from data instead of following hand-written rules
A measurable property of the data used as input to a model
A number that controls how much influence a feature has on the model's output
The score boundary above which the model triggers an action (e.g. block a transaction)
The dataset a model learns from — its patterns become the model's patterns
When the model says fraud but the transaction was actually safe
When the model says safe but the transaction was actually fraud

learningObjectives: Explain the difference between explicit rule-based programming and machine learning.
Identify model features and assign weights for a simple fraud detection task.
Set and evaluate a decision threshold using training and unseen transactions.
Recognize at least one fairness risk when models are trained on limited or biased data.
fullDescription: Age group: 12-17. Duration: 30 minutes. SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Learning goal: students can explain machine learning basics through fraud detection, assign feature weights and thresholds, and analyze false positives, false negatives, and fairness implications.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary on the board and keep it visible all lesson. Draw the training transaction table (transactions 1–4) on the board or project it. Have paper and pencils ready for group work. Keep the test transactions (5–7) hidden until Round 3.
steps: Phase 1 (0-2 min): Ask spark question on how banks detect fraud even when thieves use normal-looking transactions and valid credentials.
Phase 1 hook: connect decision scale to modern payment networks where static IF/THEN rules are insufficient.
Phase 2 (2-8 min): Feature extraction mini-demo with an everyday object. Move from vague description to measurable properties and introduce feature weights.
Phase 2 bridge: contrast explicit rules with machine learning. Humans provide examples, model learns scoring patterns.
Phase 3 (8-24 min): Fraud detector activity using 4-row training data and weighted scoring with threshold decisions.
Round 1 (3 min): identify strongest signal and discuss why feature combinations often matter more than any single feature.
Round 2 (8 min): groups assign weights for Overseas, Late Night (11pm-5am), and Amount > EUR500, then set a blocking threshold and classify all training cases.
Round 3 (5 min): apply model to unseen transactions 5 to 7 and compute false positives and false negatives.
Phase 4 discussion (24-28 min): evaluate who bears error costs and what fairness obligations follow from model limitations.
Exit reflection (28-30 min): students contrast when machine learning is more powerful than explicit rules and when it is more dangerous.
Fast finisher: black-box explainability prompt on right-to-explanation trade-offs in automated financial decisions.
discussionPrompts: Who should define acceptable error rates: company, regulator, or affected users?
How can demographic disparities emerge even when protected attributes are not explicit features?
Does more data always improve fairness, or can larger biased datasets scale injustice?
If historical labels include human bias, what exactly does the model learn and how would we detect it?
Should a bank be allowed to block transactions it cannot explain in human terms?
tips: Ask groups to justify each weight assignment with reasoning, not trial-and-error only.
Keep false-positive and false-negative costs grounded in lived consequences.
Highlight that unseen-case errors are expected with tiny training sets.
Prompt cross-lesson transfer from postcode bias in Lesson 5 to proxy bias in fraud scoring.
Frame fairness as both technical and governance work, not just model tuning.

teacherGuide: Core message: machine learning replaces hand-written rules with data-driven scoring, which scales power and risk together. Models can outperform static rules on complex patterns but inherit limits and biases from training data and label processes. Keep students focused on three levers: feature choice, weight assignment, and threshold policy.

studentExercises.title: Feature Extraction and Weighting
studentExercises.description: Identify measurable features and reason about relative importance through weights.
instructions: List measurable features for a familiar object.
Select the most informative features for classification.
Assign tentative importance weights.
Explain why some features deserve higher weights.
Connect feature choice to model blind spots.
successCriteria: Students propose measurable rather than vague features.
Students justify at least one high-weight feature.
Students recognize that chosen features constrain learning.

studentExercises.title: Train the Fraud Scorer
studentExercises.description: Build a weighted fraud score and threshold that classifies the 4 training transactions correctly.
instructions: Assign weights (1-10) for Overseas, Late Night, and Amount > EUR500.
Set block threshold based on weighted sum.
Run model on transactions 1 to 4.
Adjust until all training examples are classified as expected.
Document rationale for final weights and threshold.
successCriteria: Group produces a complete weighted scoring rule.
Group classifies all training examples correctly.
Group provides reasoning for parameter choices.

studentExercises.title: Generalization and Error Analysis
studentExercises.description: Test the trained model on unseen transactions and analyze false positives and false negatives.
instructions: Apply your model to transactions 5, 6, and 7.
Compare predictions with actual outcomes.
Count false positives and false negatives.
Choose which error type to reduce first and justify for both bank and customer perspectives.
Propose one model adjustment and predict its side effect.
successCriteria: Group differentiates false positive and false negative impacts.
Group explains trade-offs of attempted improvements.

studentExercises.title: Exit Reflection — Power vs Risk
studentExercises.description: Write one sentence contrasting where machine learning outperforms explicit rules and where it introduces danger.
instructions: Complete: Machine learning is more powerful than explicit rules when ______, but more dangerous when ______.
Include one concrete lesson example in each blank.
Mention either data quality, explainability, or fairness.
Share one revised sentence aloud.
successCriteria: Student contrasts complexity-handling benefit with risk source.
Student references a concrete model behavior from class.
Response links model danger to human consequences.

studentChallenges.title: Proxy Bias Investigation
studentChallenges.description: Trace how demographic disparities can emerge through correlated variables when protected attributes are absent.
instructions: Name one non-protected feature that could act as a proxy for demographic group membership.
Explain one pathway from proxy feature to unequal false positive rates.
Propose one audit metric to detect this issue.
State one remediation action and its trade-off.

studentChallenges.title: Explainability vs Accuracy
studentChallenges.description: Debate whether systems should trade some predictive performance for clearer human explanations in high-impact decisions.
instructions: Argue for an explanation requirement in automated banking decisions.
Describe one model-design choice that improves explainability.
Describe one potential accuracy cost of that choice.
Recommend a policy balance and justify it.
successCriteria: Student frames explainability as a design choice with costs.
Student identifies at least one technical lever for transparency.
Student proposes a defensible policy position.

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
whatToDo: Write these seven words on the board — leave them visible all lesson:
Machine learning: a system that learns patterns from data instead of following hand-written rules
Feature: a measurable property of the data used as input to a model
Weight: a number that controls how much influence a feature has on the output
Threshold: the score boundary above which the model triggers an action
Training data: the dataset a model learns from — its patterns become the model's patterns
False positive: model says fraud, but the transaction was actually safe
False negative: model says safe, but the transaction was actually fraud
Draw the training transaction table on the board (transactions 1–4):
Transaction 1: Local, €8, 9:00 AM → Safe
Transaction 2: Local, €1200, 2:00 PM → Safe
Transaction 3: Overseas, €8, 3:00 AM → Fraud
Transaction 4: Overseas, €420, 3:15 AM → Fraud
Keep transactions 5–7 hidden until Round 3.
Have paper and pencils ready for group work.

lessonSlides.title: Fei-Fei Li
lessonSlides.roleModel: Fei-Fei Li (born 1976) is a computer scientist and professor at Stanford University. She grew up in China, immigrated to the United States as a teenager, and worked in dry cleaners and restaurants to support her family while studying physics and computer science. In 2009, she created ImageNet — a dataset of over 14 million labelled images organised into more than 20,000 categories. At the time, most AI researchers focused on building cleverer algorithms. Fei-Fei Li asked a different question: what if the algorithm is not the bottleneck — what if the data is? ImageNet proved her right. Models trained on ImageNet dramatically outperformed everything before them, not because the algorithms were new, but because the data was better, larger, and more diverse. Her work launched the modern deep learning revolution and established a principle students will use today: the quality and diversity of your training data matters more than the cleverness of your model.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Introduce Fei-Fei Li simply: 'Today's role model is a scientist who changed AI — not by writing a better algorithm, but by building a better dataset. Her name is Fei-Fei Li. She created ImageNet — 14 million images, all labelled by hand — and proved that the data you train on matters more than how smart your code is. Today, you are going to learn why that matters, because you are going to train a model yourselves.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: ImageNet did not just improve one product. It triggered an entire revolution in AI. Every modern image recognition system — from face unlock on your phone to medical scans detecting cancer — traces back to the principle Fei-Fei Li proved: data quality and diversity determine model quality. When students train their fraud detector today on four transactions, they are experiencing the same principle at miniature scale.

lessonSlides.title: What We're Doing Today
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Welcome the class and set the frame: 'Today you are going to learn how computers learn — not from instructions, but from examples. You are going to build a fraud detector. You will decide what the model pays attention to, how much weight each clue gets, and where the line is between safe and fraud. And then you will test it — and find out what happens when your model meets data it has never seen before.'
whatToDo: Point to the vocabulary on the board: 'These seven words are what we're working with today. You don't need to know them yet — by the end you will.'
Point to the transaction table: 'Those are your training examples. Your model will learn from those four transactions. That is all it gets.'
Let that sink in. Four transactions is deliberately tiny — the tension is part of the lesson.
This lesson is 30 minutes. Keep transitions sharp and energy high.

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Ask the class: 'How does a bank detect a fraudulent transaction — in less than one second — when the thief is using a real card with the correct PIN?'
whatToDo: Give students 15 seconds to think.
Take 2–3 quick answers.
Students may say: 'unusual location', 'big amount', 'strange time.' All valid.

Say: 'You just described features — measurable clues that a system can use to make a decision. But here is the problem: how do you write a rule for every possible fraud pattern? You can't. There are too many patterns, and they keep changing. That is why banks use machine learning — a system that learns patterns from examples instead of being told every rule.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If the class is quiet, try: 'You are on holiday in Japan. You buy a coffee at 3 AM. Your bank blocks your card. Why? What clues did the system use? Was it the right decision?'

lessonSlides.title: Features, Weights, and the Shift to ML
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Before we train a model, we need to understand features. A feature is a measurable property — something the model can use as a clue. Let me show you.'
whatToDo: Pick up an everyday object — a pen, a water bottle, a backpack.
Ask: 'How would you describe this to a computer? Remember — a computer cannot see or guess.'
Students will say vague things: 'it is blue', 'it is big.' Push for measurable: 'How big? In centimetres? Grams? How many colours?'
Write 3–4 measurable features on the board: length (cm), weight (g), colour count, has clip (yes/no).
Say: 'These are features. A model uses features to make decisions. But not all features are equally useful. Some are strong signals, some are noise.'
Introduce weights: 'A weight tells the model how much to trust each feature. High weight = strong clue. Low weight = weak clue.'

Now bridge to ML: 'In the old approach — explicit rules — a human writes every IF/THEN rule by hand. IF overseas AND night AND big amount THEN block. But what happens when fraudsters change their patterns?'

Answer: 'The rules break. Machine learning is different. Instead of writing rules, you give the system examples — here is fraud, here is safe — and it learns the patterns. You choose the features and the model learns the weights.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Payment networks like Visa process over 65,000 transactions per second. Each one needs a fraud decision in under 100 milliseconds. No human can write enough IF/THEN rules to cover every pattern. Machine learning models learn from billions of historical transactions and adapt as fraud patterns evolve.
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If features feel abstract, try: 'Imagine you are sorting apples. What features would you use? Colour? Size? Weight? Bruises? Now imagine sorting 10,000 apples per minute — you need a system that does it automatically. That system needs features and weights.'

lessonSlides.title: Spot the Strongest Signal
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Look at the four training transactions on the board. Your first task: which single feature is the strongest signal for fraud? You have 3 minutes to discuss in your groups.'
whatToDo: Point to the transaction table. Ask groups to inspect the four rows.
After 2 minutes, take answers. Most groups will say 'location' — both fraud cases are overseas, both safe cases are local.
Push: 'Is location enough on its own? Transaction 2 is local and €1200 — that is a big amount but it is safe. If you only used amount, you would flag it as fraud. That is a false positive.'
Say: 'No single feature is enough. Fraud is about combinations — overseas AND late night AND unusual amount. That is why models use weighted combinations, not single rules.'
Close: 'Now you are going to build your own weighted model.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If groups just say 'overseas = fraud', challenge: 'What about someone who travels for work and buys things overseas every week? Is every overseas transaction fraud? How does your model tell the difference?'

lessonSlides.title: Feature Extraction and Weighting
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'List measurable features for a familiar object. Then assign weights — how important is each feature for classifying that object?'
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 2.
Students practise the concept of features and weights on everyday objects before applying it to fraud detection.

lessonSlides.title: Train the Fraud Scorer
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Now you are going to train a fraud detection model. In groups of three, you need to assign weights and set a threshold. Here are your three features.'
whatToDo: Write the three features on the board:
Feature 1: Is Overseas? (yes/no)
Feature 2: Is Late Night? (between 11 PM and 5 AM) (yes/no)
Feature 3: Amount > €500? (yes/no)

Explain: 'Assign a weight from 1 to 10 for each feature. If the feature is true, multiply by the weight. Add up the total score. Then set a threshold: if the score is at or above your threshold, the model blocks the transaction.'

Give an example: 'If Overseas = weight 8, Late Night = weight 5, Amount > €500 = weight 3, and a transaction is Overseas (8) and Late Night (5) but not high amount (0), the score is 13. If your threshold is 10, this transaction gets blocked.'
Give groups 8 minutes to work. They must classify all 4 training transactions correctly.
Walk around. Push groups to justify their weights: 'Why did you give Overseas a 9? What evidence from the training data supports that?'
After 8 minutes, ask two groups to share their weights and thresholds. Likely they will differ — that is the point.
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If a group gets stuck, try: 'Start with the clearest pattern. Both fraud transactions are overseas. Both safe transactions are local. What does that tell you about the weight for Overseas?'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Fei-Fei Li's core finding applies directly: label quality determines model quality. The features you weight and the labels you assign are the model's entire view of reality.

lessonSlides.title: Train the Fraud Scorer
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Assign weights (1–10) for each feature. Set a threshold. Classify all four training transactions. All four must be correct before you move on.'
rules: Each feature gets a weight from 1 to 10.
If a feature is true for a transaction, add its weight to the score.
If the total score meets or exceeds your threshold, block the transaction.
All four training transactions must be classified correctly.
Document your reasoning — not just the numbers.
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 3 Round 2.
See the Phase 3 Round 2 slide for full facilitation instructions.

lessonSlides.title: Test on Unseen Data
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Your model works perfectly on the training data. But does it work on data it has never seen? Let's find out.'
whatToDo: Reveal the three test transactions on the board:
Transaction 5: Local, €650, 3:30 AM → Actual: Safe
Transaction 6: Overseas, €25, 2:00 PM → Actual: Safe
Transaction 7: Overseas, €800, 4:00 AM → Actual: Fraud
Say: 'Apply your model to these three. Score each one and decide: block or allow.'
Give groups 3 minutes.
Then reveal the actual outcomes. Most models will get at least one wrong.
Transaction 5 is the trap: it is local (likely low overseas weight) but late night and high amount — many models will block it. It is actually safe. That is a false positive.
Transaction 6 is tricky: it is overseas (high weight) but daytime and low amount. Many models will block it. It is actually safe. Another false positive.
Ask: 'What went wrong? Your model was perfect on training data but made errors on new data. Why?'

Answer: 'Because four training examples are not enough to learn every pattern. The model overfitted — it learned the specific examples, not the general rules. This is exactly what Fei-Fei Li proved: small, narrow data creates narrow models.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If groups get everything right, push: 'You got lucky — or you built a conservative model. What if there were 100 test transactions instead of 3? Would your model still work? What patterns might appear that your four training examples never showed?'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Li proved that a model trained on too narrow a dataset fails to generalise. The gap between your training accuracy and test accuracy is the exact phenomenon she documented at scale.

lessonSlides.title: Generalization and Error Analysis
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Apply your model to the three unseen transactions. Count your false positives and false negatives. Which error is worse — and for whom?'
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 3 Round 3.
See the Phase 3 Round 3 slide for full facilitation instructions.

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Pick the question below that feels most alive based on how the class went.
One strong discussion is better than two rushed ones.
Give students a moment to think before taking answers.
discussionQuestions: 'Your model blocked a safe transaction. Who pays the cost — the bank or the customer?' Follow-up: 'What if it is a tourist buying medicine abroad at 3 AM? Is a false positive just an inconvenience, or can it be harmful?'
'Your model let a fraud transaction through. Who pays the cost now?' Follow-up: 'Is a false negative always worse than a false positive? For whom?'
'Who should set the threshold — the bank's engineering team, a government regulator, or the customers themselves? What happens when their interests conflict?'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If discussion is slow, try: 'Imagine your card gets blocked every time you travel. You call the bank. They say: our model flagged your transaction. You ask why. They say: we cannot tell you — it is a black box. Is that acceptable?'

lessonSlides.title: Exit Reflection — Power vs Risk
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Final task. On your own, complete this sentence: Machine learning is more powerful than explicit rules when ______, but more dangerous when ______.'

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Ask every student to write and complete the sentence on paper or sticky note.
They must include one concrete example from the lesson in each blank.
They must reference data quality, explainability, or fairness.
Collect papers or stick them on the board.
Read 2–3 strong responses aloud.
lessonSlides.exitReflection: Machine learning is more powerful than explicit rules when ______, but more dangerous when ______.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Close with: 'Fei-Fei Li proved that the data is the model. You trained on four transactions — and the model learned exactly those four patterns. When it met something new, it stumbled. Next lesson, we are going to ask what happens when the data itself is unfair — and how you audit a system for bias.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If reflections are shallow, push: 'Do not just say ML is powerful for big data. Be specific. What did your model do that an IF/THEN rule could not? And what did your model get wrong that a human would have caught? Name the transactions.'
